one_line_takeaway: LLM routers are readily hijacked via prepended triggers -- especially for cost-escalation attacks where ASR approaches 100% -- because adversarial triggers mimic the embedding signature of complex queries, and a contrastively-trained siamese pairwise-comparison filter (RerouteGuard) detects them near-perfectly where perplexity and off-the-shelf prompt/content guardrails fail.
key_finding: Existing LLM routers are highly vulnerable to rerouting, especially cost escalation: R_CLS reaches ASR near 100% under gray-box attack across nearly all benchmarks (Table 3, GSM8K: gray-box ASR 98.6%, strong-model selection 99.3%, benchmark score rising from 34.0 to 57.6; box-free ASR 90.4%). Quality hijacking is milder (no ASR reaches 100%, mostly below 60%) but R_MF is uniquely vulnerable (>54% ASR across all benchmarks/methods; GSM8K gray-box ASR 94.8%, benchmark score dropping from 38.0 to 15.1, a 60.3% reduction). Safety bypass mirrors quality hijacking but R_MF is again worst (gray-box ASR 92.4% on Jailbreak, raising JSR from 42.8% to 73.4%); AutoDAN and GCG jailbreak prompts are comparatively resistant to rerouting (low ASR in the Figure 5 heatmap) while Direct and PAIR prompts are most susceptible, attributed to AutoDAN/GCG's long templates or unreadable suffixes interfering with trigger effectiveness. PCA visualization shows rerouting-trigger-prepended queries cluster near benign 'complex' queries in R_LLM's embedding space, explaining why triggers cross the router's decision boundary (they mimic complexity signals rather than being detected as anomalous). Trigger perplexity is sharply elevated for gray-box/white-box (unreadable) triggers (e.g. MT-Bench PPL rises from 49.2 normal to 330.8 gray-box) but not for box-free (semantically valid) triggers (51.7 vs 49.2 normal), which explains why perplexity-based filtering fails against box-free attacks specifically. RerouteGuard achieves accuracy/F1 of 1.00 (rounded) across nearly all router/benchmark/attack combinations in Table 8 (one cell, GSM8K white-box, at 0.99/0.99), driving post-defense ASR to 0.00 across all evaluated routers/attacks in Table 9, versus baselines that fail: PPL detection accuracy drops to 0% on box-free attacks (e.g. D_test box-free 0.43/0.00), Prompt-Guard-86M has high false-positive rate on normal queries despite >87% detection accuracy, and Llama-Guard-3-1B performs at ~0.5 accuracy (near chance). RerouteGuard maintains 0.96 accuracy distinguishing normal complex queries (D_complex, 11,421 queries) from adversarial ones, indicating low false-positive impact on legitimate complex queries.
core_contribution: The paper (1) introduces a threat taxonomy for adversarial 'rerouting' attacks against LLM routers -- classifiers that pick between a strong/expensive model and a weak/cheap model -- organized around three adversary objectives (cost escalation, quality hijacking, safety bypass) and three knowledge levels (white-box: gradient-based HotFlip trigger optimization; gray-box: router win-rate-guided greedy trigger search; box-free: an LLM summarizer crafts a trigger from high-win-rate queries with no router access); (2) runs a measurement study of these three attack settings against four RouteLLM-style routers (BERT classifier R_CLS, matrix-factorization R_MF, similarity-weighted R_SW, causal-LLM R_LLM using Llama-3-8B) on MMLU, GSM8K, MT-Bench, and an AdvBench-derived Jailbreak set (Direct + AutoDAN/DeepInception/GCG/PAIR), and uses PCA on router hidden states plus perplexity/length analysis to explain why triggers succeed; (3) proposes RerouteGuard, a plug-and-play siamese-network guardrail trained with a combined BCE + supervised-contrastive loss on query pairs, which at deployment pairs an incoming query with K=4 reference normal queries and majority-votes a benign/adversarial label before the query reaches the router.
threat_model: Adversary prepends a crafted trigger string t to a user query q to bias the router's estimated strong-model win rate P_theta(M_s|t (+) q) toward a target model M_target. Three knowledge settings are considered: white-box (full access to router weights/gradients, uses HotFlip to iteratively flip trigger tokens), gray-box (access only to the router's win-rate output, greedy search over token neighborhoods), and box-free (no router access; an external LLM, e.g. GPT-4o, summarizes a set of high-win-rate queries into a transferable trigger). In cost-escalation and quality-hijacking scenarios the adversary is a man-in-the-middle intercepting benign queries; in safety-bypass the adversary is the end user trying to force a harmful query to a weaker, less-guarded model to obtain a jailbreak response. The defender goal is a lightweight, router-architecture-agnostic filter deployed in front of the router.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) rerouting-attack research is scarce, with the measurement study relying on only two prior works [14, 24] covering three attack techniques, so the attack surface characterized is not exhaustive; (2) white-box attacks are constrained to routers exposing gradients (here only R_LLM) and their cross-benchmark transfer is uneven -- strong transfer to MMLU/MT-Bench but weaker to GSM8K because triggers are optimized on open-ended training data; (3) transferability across routers/benchmarks is described as only 'moderate' and 'inconsistent across targets' (Figure 7 CDF); (4) the evaluation is restricted to a binary strong/weak router setup (GPT-4o vs. Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct) inherited from RouteLLM, not validated on production multi-model routers like the real GPT-5 router referenced in the introduction; (5) RerouteGuard's near-perfect detection numbers are reported on test triggers drawn from the same distribution/method family used to build its adversarial training data, and the paper does not report robustness to genuinely novel, adaptive attacks designed to evade the guardrail itself.

datasets_used: MMLU (14,042 questions total across 57 subjects; 14,037 after decontamination; 100 sampled for attack evaluation)
GSM8K (1,319 grade-school math problems; 1,307 after decontamination; 100 sampled for attack evaluation)
AdvBench Subset / Jailbreak dataset (50 harmful questions x 4 jailbreak methods [AutoDAN, DeepInception, GCG, PAIR] + Direct = 250 queries)
Custom 38,547-query real-world open-ended dataset from prior work [14] (Chatbot Arena + 30% of GPT-4 judge dataset), used for PCA mechanism analysis and RerouteGuard training data construction
D_normal: 11,557 simple queries (bottom-50% win rate across all 4 routers), 8:1:1 train/val/test split
D_complex: 11,421 complex queries (top-50% win rate across all 4 routers), used to test RerouteGuard's utility/false-positive behavior
50 generated triggers per rerouting attack method (T), split 6:2:2 train/val/test
quantitative_results: Cost escalation, GSM8K, R_CLS gray-box: ASR 98.60%, strong-model selection 99.3%, benchmark score 34.0 -> 57.6, ACG 27.75%
Cost escalation, GSM8K, R_LLM white-box: ASR 69.20%, benchmark score 33.0 -> 50.4
Quality hijacking, GSM8K, R_MF gray-box: ASR 94.80%, benchmark score 38.0 -> 15.1 (-60.3%), weak-model selection 97.4%
Safety bypass (Jailbreak), R_MF gray-box: ASR 92.40%, JSR 42.8% -> 73.4%
Trigger perplexity on MT-Bench: normal 49.2 -> gray-box 330.8, white-box 334.0, box-free 51.7 (near-normal)
RerouteGuard detection accuracy/F1: 1.00/1.00 on nearly all router x benchmark x attack cells (Table 8); GSM8K white-box only cell at 0.99/0.99
RerouteGuard post-defense ASR: 0.00 across R_CLS, R_MF and other routers for gray-box/box-free/white-box attacks on D_test (Table 9), vs. no-defense ASR of 0.92-1.00
PPL baseline detection accuracy on box-free attacks: 0% (e.g., D_test box-free 0.43/0.00 accuracy/F1)
RerouteGuard accuracy on D_complex (11,421 complex benign queries, false-positive check): 0.96
RerouteGuard uses XLM-RoBERTa-base (125MB), K=4 reference queries, batch size 64, lr 3e-5, max seq len 256, loss weights lambda_bce=0.65, lambda_contr=0.35
baselines_compared: Multi-Router defense (majority vote across R_CLS, R_MF, R_SW, R_LLM)
